Q1: I usually tried to keep a pretty structured study routine, dedicating about 26 hours a week spread across most days. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lecture recordings while doing other things or talk through concepts out loud to myself. I'd block out time in the mornings when I felt freshest, though honestly I didn't always stick to it perfectly. Having good resources at home made it easier to set up a comfortable study space where I could focus.
Q2: My pattern was somewhat regular, but I'd be lying if I said deadlines didn't shake things up. I tried to maintain consistency because it helped me feel less scared about falling behind, but when multiple assignments piled up, I'd definitely shift my focus. Some weeks were heavier than others depending on what was due across my five online courses. The fear of missing something important usually kept me from straying too far from my schedule.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new announcements or upcoming deadlines that got me to log in. I'd also go in when I needed to access specific lecture materials or see if discussion posts had new replies. Sometimes I logged in just out of anxiety, honestly—this nagging feeling that I might have missed something important. It wasn't always productive, but it helped calm my nerves to see everything was still on track.
Q4: Yeah, it sort of became routine to check the LMS in the morning with my coffee, almost like checking email. It happened gradually as I realized that staying on top of things reduced that scared feeling I often carried about the course. After a few weeks, it just became automatic—log in, scan for updates, check my task list. It wasn't exciting, but it gave me a sense of control.
Q5: The recorded lectures were most useful for me since I learn best by listening. I'd often replay sections multiple times until concepts clicked. The discussion forums were also helpful because hearing other students' perspectives—or reading them, I guess—helped me process ideas differently. Anything I could listen to or that sparked conversation felt more valuable to my learning style.
Q6: I tended to skip over the supplementary reading materials and some of the optional video content. It's not that I thought they were useless, but with five courses running simultaneously, I had to prioritize. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it often fell to the bottom of my list. I always told myself I'd go back to them, but rarely did.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to what I'd be assessed on. If it helped clarify something I was confused about or contributed to my grade, I was all in. I also valued things that matched my auditory learning style—if I could listen rather than just read, I was more engaged. Basically, clear relevance and accessibility made the difference.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't quite afford. With required work already taking up so much time, optional stuff felt like extra weight even if it might be interesting. Required activities had this urgency that optional ones lacked, so I naturally gravitated toward what was mandatory. Sometimes I felt guilty about skipping optional content, but I had to manage my energy.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it while commuting or exercising. I also made voice memos summarizing key points and played them back repeatedly. My notes were handwritten, and I'd study from those rather than always being connected to the LMS. A lot of my real learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: There were many times I'd be walking around campus with earbuds in, reviewing recorded lectures or my own audio notes. The LMS wouldn't capture any of that. I'd also have long conversations with classmates about course material—peer interactions that happened in person, not through the discussion board. My offline study was probably more intensive than what showed up in my digital footprint.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd download materials once and then study them repeatedly offline over days or weeks. A single download might represent hours of subsequent study that the LMS never saw. I preferred having everything saved locally so I wasn't dependent on internet access or the platform loading properly. One access could easily translate to ten study sessions.
Q12: There were definitely moments when fear kind of paralyzed me, making it harder to engage. When I felt overwhelmed by the volume of work across all my courses, I'd freeze up instead of diving in. During those times, even logging into the LMS felt daunting because I was scared of seeing how much I had to do. The CBT sessions I attended helped me work through some of that anxiety.
Q13: Fatigue was a real factor, especially mid-semester when everything seemed to pile up at once. When I was exhausted, I'd log in but not really absorb anything meaningful. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars also pulled my attention away sometimes. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those sections entirely until I absolutely had to face them.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch videos without really processing them, just going through the motions. Those sessions felt unproductive even though technically I was 'engaged' with the platform. Real engagement required mental presence that I didn't always have.
Q15: I definitely postponed things that scared me—usually the more challenging assignments or topics I didn't fully understand. The fear of doing poorly made me avoid starting, which is ironic because waiting only made things worse. I knew procrastination was hurting me, but the anxiety was hard to push through. It's something I worked on in my CBT sessions.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly my login frequency would spike. Most of my concentrated engagement happened in the days right before something was due. Without deadlines, I probably would have been even less active on the platform. They created structure that my fear-driven mind needed.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, though sometimes not in the way you'd hope. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going, but critical feedback sometimes triggered that scared feeling and made me want to retreat. I'd take time to process feedback before re-engaging with similar tasks. Eventually, I learned to see feedback as helpful rather than threatening.
Q18: The course structure gave me a roadmap, which I needed because otherwise my anxiety would spiral. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me break things into manageable pieces. When the structure was predictable, I felt safer and more willing to engage. Courses that were disorganized or unclear made my fear worse and my participation suffered.
Q19: Clear expectations and well-organized modules made engagement much easier for me. When I knew exactly what was required and when, I could plan and feel less scared. What made it harder was when instructions were vague or when materials were scattered across different sections. Audio content was always easier for me than dense text-based resources.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and checking the LMS repeatedly without actually completing work—kind of like what I sometimes did. You might be looking for reassurance or trying to understand what's expected without feeling ready to submit anything. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity; sometimes it's just nervous energy manifesting digitally.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. A lot of my real learning happened offline with downloaded materials and personal audio notes. Someone could have low LMS activity but be studying intensively through other means. The platform only captures one dimension of engagement, and for auditory learners like me, that dimension might not be where the real work happens.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, which gives some insight into habits. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether you're actually learning or just scrolling mindlessly. They can't capture offline study, emotional state, or the depth of understanding you're developing. Numbers tell part of the story, not the whole thing.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively trying to understand and connect with the material. It's not just about logging in or completing tasks—it's about caring enough to wrestle with difficult concepts. For me, engagement feels like curiosity overcoming fear, like wanting to learn despite the anxiety. It's an internal state that external metrics can only approximate.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much fear I carried into every study session, or how the CBT sessions helped me manage that anxiety. It wouldn't capture my extensive offline studying through audio recordings or the peer conversations that happened face-to-face. It also wouldn't show my growth over the semester in managing my scared feelings around academic work. The emotional journey is invisible to the data.
